nodes.label: Culture & Change Implementation
nodes.description: Analyze how organizational culture affects change implementation, distinguishing structural from individual explanations.
misconceptions: Culture change is primarily a communication/messaging problem
Resistance to change is irrational or reflects poor attitude
Culture can be directly managed through top-down directives

nodes.label: Structural vs. Individual Explanations
nodes.description: The distinction between explaining organizational behavior through individual traits/actions (leadership, motivation, attitude) versus structural features (incentives, systems, power dynamics).
misconceptions: Defaulting to 'leadership failed' as the explanation for any organizational problem
Treating individual behavior as independent of structural context

nodes.label: Mental Models (Senge)
nodes.description: Deeply held internal images of how the world works that shape how people interpret events and take action — often unconscious and resistant to change.
misconceptions: Equating mental models with opinions or beliefs (they're deeper — structural, often invisible)
Assuming surfacing a mental model is sufficient to change it

nodes.label: Psychological Safety (Edmondson)
nodes.description: A shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — speaking up, admitting mistakes, challenging the status quo without fear of punishment.
misconceptions: Confusing psychological safety with 'being nice' or avoiding conflict
Thinking psychological safety means no accountability

nodes.label: Schein's Three Levels of Culture
nodes.description: Artifacts (visible structures and processes), espoused values (stated strategies, goals, philosophies), and underlying assumptions (unconscious, taken-for-granted beliefs).
misconceptions: Confusing espoused values with actual culture (what we say vs. what we do)
Treating artifacts as the whole of culture

nodes.label: Systems Thinking
nodes.description: Understanding organizations as complex systems with feedback loops, emergent properties, and non-linear causation.
misconceptions: Linear causal thinking (A causes B, so fix A to fix B)
Ignoring feedback loops and unintended consequences

nodes.label: Resistance as Rational Behavior
nodes.description: Reframing resistance not as irrational stubbornness but as rational behavior given existing incentive structures, power dynamics, and mental models.
misconceptions: Resistance = bad attitude or fear of the unknown
If people understood the change, they would support it

nodes.label: Incentive Structures
nodes.description: How formal and informal reward systems shape behavior — and how misalignment between stated goals and actual incentives undermines change.
misconceptions: Incentives = compensation (they include status, workload, autonomy, social capital)
Assuming stated incentives match actual incentives

nodes.label: Change Management Strategy
nodes.description: Designing change implementation strategies that account for culture, structure, and individual factors.

nodes.label: Organizational Design
nodes.description: Understanding how formal structures, roles, and processes shape behavior and culture.

edges.description: Must distinguish structural from individual explanations before analyzing resistance as rational structural behavior.
The foundational lens for analyzing culture's role in change.
Understanding mental models is required to see why people interpret the same change differently.
Culture operates partly through shared mental models.
Psychological safety determines whether concerns about change surface or stay hidden.
Must analyze culture at all three levels to understand how it affects change.
Systems thinking enables seeing resistance as emergent from structural conditions.
Change implementation requires understanding non-linear effects and feedback loops.
Resistance is often rational given existing incentives.
Understanding resistance as rational behavior is required before analyzing culture-change dynamics.
Culture analysis feeds into change strategy design.
Understanding culture-change dynamics informs organizational design choices.
